skippedTestBreakdown.individualTestsDisabled: at least 44 (25 skip call-sites; tests/integration/connectors/erp.test.ts:24 is a single describe.skip wrapping 19 individual it() cases)

meta.tsIgnoreBreakdown: 1 occurrence in packages (packages/domain/src/security/__tests__/AISecurityEventTypes.test.ts) + 32 occurrences across 20 files in apps (@ts-ignore / @ts-expect-error combined)
meta.eslintDisableBreakdown: 12 occurrences across 6 files in packages, 37 occurrences across 24 files in apps
meta.summary: Read-only static audit of the ENG-OPS-002 baseline worktree. The most consequential finding is systemic: 21 fast-check property tests across 9 files under tests/property/unit/ use it.skip/test.skip to document confirmed, unfixed production bugs (RACE-PURE-01/03/08/09/10/11/13/M1/M2, RACE-WORKE-04, PROTO-ROUTE-01, RACE-DEDUP-07a/07b/07c) rather than either fixing the bug or gating merge on it — several skip comments state outright that the test is 'marked skip to keep the file green'. ADR-054 claims a `no-skipped-tests` ESLint rule is 'already enforced in CI', but no such rule exists anywhere in eslint.config.mjs, so nothing currently prevents this pattern from spreading. Two of the skipped dedup-evaluator bugs (RACE-DEDUP-07a/07c) are live correctness gaps in lead/contact/account duplicate detection (false negatives and false positives respectively). Separately, a full 19-test SAP ERP adapter suite (tests/integration/connectors/erp.test.ts) and 3 of 6 field-encryption tests are disabled outright. Structural findings include four 2500+-line files (two 'use client' detail pages mixing data-fetching/business-logic/rendering, two tRPC god-routers), a security-relevant code fork (prompt-sanitizer.ts duplicated between apps/api and packages/adapters with a silently diverging regex), tests asserting on Prisma mock-call shape instead of returned outcomes (over-mocking), two literal expect(true).toBe(true) placeholder assertions, three-file test-suite proliferation for two small seed scripts, and one dead/orphaned tRPC procedure export (loggedProcedure) with zero real consumers.

findings.impact: Five distinct confirmed bugs in Ticket domain state-machine guards (priority mutation and assign/unassign on terminal ARCHIVED/CLOSED tickets, negative SLA-pause duration) ship un-fixed and un-regression-tested. A ticket that should be immutable once ARCHIVED can still have its priority or assignee changed in production; nothing in CI would catch a regression here because the test that proves the bug is skipped rather than failing.
findings.rootCause: Property-based audit (ADR-054 Phase 0) found real domain bugs; rather than fixing TicketConstants.ts guards or filing tracked follow-up tasks, the reproducing tests were committed as it.skip so the suite stays green.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-054 flaky-test policy: "Skipping (test.skip) requires a linked issue" — these skips reference internal audit finding IDs (RACE-PURE-10/11/M1) from race-condition-findings.json, not tracked GitHub issues or Sprint_plan.csv tasks.
findings.remediation: Either fix the isTerminalStatus() guard usage in Ticket.changePriority/assign/unassign (packages/domain ticket aggregate) and un-skip, or file individual Sprint tasks for each RACE-PURE-10/11/M1 finding and reference the task ID in the skip reason per ADR-054.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -n "test.skip(" tests/property/unit/crm/task-domain.prop.test.ts` → lines 271, 284, 302, 309, 315. Titles: 'RACE-PURE-09: changeStatus("ARCHIVED", user) on IN_PROGRESS task must be rejected (transition table not consulted)', 'RACE-PURE-09: complete() on a PENDING task ... must be rejected', 'RACE-PURE-M2: assignToLead()/assignToContact()/assignToOpportunity() on COMPLETED/CANCELLED/ARCHIVED task must be rejected'.
findings.impact: Task aggregate's state machine does not consult a transition table before status changes or assignment, so terminal/invalid task states can still be mutated (e.g. re-assigning a CANCELLED task, completing a task that never started). Silent data-integrity drift in the task domain used across leads/contacts/deals workflows.
findings.rootCause: Same pattern as QUAL-001: audit-discovered bug documented via skipped test instead of fixed or tracked.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-054 flaky-test policy (linked-issue requirement for skips).
findings.remediation: Introduce VALID_TASK_TRANSITIONS validation in Task.changeStatus/assignTo* (packages/domain task aggregate) and un-skip these five tests; or file tracked tasks per finding ID.

findings.impact: JS falsy-coercion bug (`threshold || 100`) in duplicate-rule-evaluator.ts means any dedup rule configured with threshold=0 ("match everything") silently behaves as threshold=100 ("match only exact"), causing real duplicate leads/contacts/accounts to go undetected. This is a functional correctness defect in a CRM's core dedup feature, currently masked by a skipped test rather than surfaced.
findings.rootCause: `threshold || 100` treats the valid input 0 as falsy; the property test that proves the defect was skipped instead of the one-line fix (`threshold ?? 100`) being applied.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-054 flaky-test policy (linked-issue requirement for skips); ADR-050 (duplicate-detection runtime) implicitly assumes dedup rules behave as configured.
findings.remediation: Change `threshold || 100` to `threshold ?? 100` in duplicate-rule-evaluator.ts's resolveFloor (referenced at line 166 per the test comment) and un-skip.

findings.impact: Two records that both lack name/company data score 100% identical on the composite field (matching on the empty '|' / '||' skeleton), producing false-positive duplicate flags. In a CRM this can incorrectly merge or block creation of genuinely distinct leads/contacts that simply have sparse data.
findings.rootCause: extractFieldValue's empty-input guard (`if (!inputValue) continue`) does not treat the composite separator skeleton as empty.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-054 flaky-test policy (linked-issue requirement for skips).
findings.remediation: Make the composite field extractor return '' when all sub-components are empty (per the comment's own fix proposal) and un-skip.

findings.impact: Conceptually incomplete self-match guard in the dedup evaluator; low real-world likelihood (UUIDs are never undefined in production) but a latent defect that a future refactor (e.g. bulk-import path constructing input objects without id) could reintroduce as a real self-match false-positive.
findings.rootCause: Guard condition `inputId && inputId === candidate.id` treats undefined===undefined as non-matching, so lacks an explicit undefined check.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-054 flaky-test policy (linked-issue requirement for skips).
findings.remediation: Add explicit undefined guard on both inputId and candidateId in duplicate-rule-evaluator.ts and un-skip.

findings.impact: Lead.changeStatus has no transition table, so a Lead can be moved LOST→NEW or UNQUALIFIED→CONVERTED — semantically invalid reverse transitions in the sales pipeline that would corrupt pipeline-stage reporting and conversion analytics. The comment states outright the skip exists 'to keep the file green', i.e. the failing invariant was known and suppressed rather than fixed.
findings.rootCause: No VALID_LEAD_TRANSITIONS enforcement exists in the Lead aggregate's changeStatus method.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-054 flaky-test policy: skip exists purely to keep CI green, the opposite of the stated 'no-flaky/no-skip' intent of the ADR.
findings.remediation: Add a VALID_LEAD_TRANSITIONS state table to the Lead domain aggregate (packages/domain/src/leads) and validate in changeStatus(); un-skip.

findings.impact: Opportunity.weightedValue (used in pipeline forecasting/revenue reporting) has a float-precision rounding bug producing off-by-one-cent discrepancies. Individually trivial, but aggregated across thousands of opportunities in forecast rollups this is a systematic (not random) under-count.
findings.rootCause: Float arithmetic (`value.amount * probability.asDecimal`) instead of integer-cents arithmetic (`Money.fromCents(Math.round(value.cents * probability.asDecimal), ...)`) per the comment's own proposed fix.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-054 flaky-test policy (linked-issue requirement for skips).
findings.remediation: Switch Opportunity.weightedValue to integer-cents Math.round arithmetic per the documented fix and un-skip.

findings.impact: packages/domain's Money value object silently rejects USD (`Money.create(10,'USD').isFailure === true`) while GBP is duplicated in the SUPPORTED_CURRENCIES array. For a CRM billing/invoicing feature, inability to construct USD money values is a significant functional gap if any US-market tenant is expected — flagged Low here only because it fails loudly (isFailure) rather than silently corrupting data.
findings.rootCause: SUPPORTED_CURRENCIES array in packages/domain/src/shared/Money.ts has a data-entry error (GBP listed twice, USD omitted).
findings.policyViolation: ADR-054 flaky-test policy (linked-issue requirement for skips).
findings.remediation: Replace SUPPORTED_CURRENCIES with ['USD','GBP','EUR','CAD','AUD','JPY'] per the comment's own proposed fix and un-skip.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -n "it.skip(" tests/property/unit/crm/payment-terms.prop.test.ts` → lines 228, 294, 433, titled 'RACE-PURE-13: calculateDueDate returns exactly daysUntilDue UTC calendar days after issueDate', '(month-boundary variant)', '(net30 variant)'.
findings.impact: Invoice due-date calculation (PaymentTerms.calculateDueDate) has an unverified UTC-day-boundary defect (RACE-PURE-13); three property variants covering the base case, month/year boundaries, and the common net30 preset are all skipped rather than fixed, so due-date correctness across month boundaries is unverified in CI.
findings.rootCause: Audit-discovered date-arithmetic defect in PaymentTerms.calculateDueDate, documented but not fixed.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-054 flaky-test policy (linked-issue requirement for skips).
findings.remediation: Investigate and fix the UTC calendar-day arithmetic in PaymentTerms.calculateDueDate (packages/domain billing) and un-skip all three variants.

findings.evidence:
REPRODUCIBLE: verbatim lines 385-391: "* used properly. The production defect is that the caller omits consumeRetry.
   * Fix: in queue-factory.ts, add
   *   globalRetryBudget.consumeRetry(QUEUE_NAMES.AI_SCORING);
   * immediately after the canRetry guard passes (before the await queue.add(...)).
   */
  test.skip('RACE-WORKE-04: budget guard is a no-op — canRetry always true because consumeRetry is never called in production caller', ...)".
findings.impact: The retry-budget ceiling for the ai-scoring queue is a no-op in production: canRetry() is checked but consumeRetry() is never called by the real caller, so the budget guard never actually decrements and can never block excess retries — meaning retry storms against AI scoring are unbounded.
findings.rootCause: Missing `globalRetryBudget.consumeRetry(...)` call in queue-factory.ts's production enqueue path, per the test's own diagnosis.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-054 flaky-test policy (linked-issue requirement for skips).
findings.remediation: Add the missing consumeRetry() call in queue-factory.ts immediately after the canRetry guard passes, per the documented fix, and un-skip.

findings.impact: budgetForRoute() indexes a plain object without an Object.hasOwn guard, so calling it with a route name that collides with an Object.prototype member (e.g. 'toString', 'constructor') returns the prototype function instead of the numeric fallback — a prototype-pollution-adjacent defect (ADR-053 N+1 query-budget detector). Low real-world likelihood since route names are typically static strings, but worth a one-line hasOwn fix.
findings.rootCause: ROUTE_BUDGETS uses `ROUTE_BUDGETS[routeName]` plain-object indexing without an own-property check.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-054 flaky-test policy (linked-issue requirement for skips).
findings.remediation: Guard budgetForRoute with `Object.hasOwn(ROUTE_BUDGETS, routeName) ? ROUTE_BUDGETS[routeName] : fallback` and un-skip.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `grep -n "no-skipped-tests" docs/architecture/adr/ADR-054-property-based-race-condition-testing.md eslint.config.mjs` returns ONE match (the ADR line itself: "commented-out tests are rejected by the `no-skipped-tests` ESLint rule already enforced in CI.") and ZERO matches in eslint.config.mjs. `grep -n "vitest" eslint.config.mjs` and `grep -n "no-disabled-tests\|vitest/" eslint.config.mjs` also return no matches — the repo's flat ESLint config has no vitest-specific plugin or rule at all.
findings.impact: ADR-054's flaky-test policy is unenforced. There is no automated gate preventing engineers from committing `it.skip`/`test.skip` calls, which is precisely how the 21 documented-bug skips in QUAL-001 through QUAL-011 entered the codebase undetected. The ADR's Status is 'Proposed' but the surrounding prose asserts current-tense enforcement ('already enforced in CI'), which is factually false against the current eslint.config.mjs.
findings.rootCause: ADR-054 (Status: Proposed) describes an eslint-plugin-vitest `no-disabled-tests`-equivalent rule as already shipped, but the implementation step was never completed even though property tests that rely on the policy (tests/property/unit/**) were already merged.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-054 self-violation: its own stated CI-enforcement claim does not match the current eslint.config.mjs.
findings.remediation: Add `eslint-plugin-vitest`'s `no-disabled-tests` (or an equivalent custom rule) to eslint.config.mjs scoped to `**/*.test.ts`, with an allow-list mechanism requiring a linked issue ID in the skip reason string, then reconcile all 21 existing bug-documenting skips (QUAL-001 through QUAL-011) either by fixing the underlying bug or filing tracked tasks.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: verbatim lines 22-24: "// Skip until SAP adapter implementation is complete - IFC-099\n// The current stub implementation doesn't match the test expectations\ndescribe.skip('SAP ERP Adapter', () => {". `grep -c "  it(" tests/integration/connectors/erp.test.ts` = 19 individual test cases wrapped inside this single describe.skip.
findings.impact: packages/adapters/src/erp/sap/client.ts (SAPAdapter, SAPAuthenticationError, SAPConnectionError, SAPRateLimitError, SAPNotFoundError) has zero executed test coverage — all 19 tests covering auth, connection errors, rate limiting, and not-found handling are disabled. Unlike the property-test skips above, this one at least references a real task ID (IFC-099), but the whole adapter ships with no regression protection.
findings.rootCause: SAP adapter implementation is a stub that predates or diverges from the intended contract; tests were written against the intended contract and disabled wholesale rather than adjusted to the stub or the stub being completed.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-054 flaky-test policy (skip requires linked issue — IFC-099 is referenced, which is the correct pattern, but the underlying work item appears stale/unresolved).
findings.remediation: Verify IFC-099's current status in Sprint_plan.csv; either complete the SAP adapter implementation to match the test contract and un-skip, or formally mark ERP/SAP connector as backlog/deferred in the sprint plan so this isn't silently carried forward.

findings.impact: 3 of the field-encryption test file's cases are disabled: the two encryption dev/prod-key-presence cases have a documented reason (env not satisfiable in isolated test process) and are lower risk since packages/db/src/client.ts:154-162 shows a bespoke fieldEncryptionExtension() is actually wired (not the removed prisma-field-encryption library), but the library-import test documents that the ORIGINAL library dependency was removed for Prisma 7 incompatibility without a tracked follow-up to confirm the bespoke replacement has equivalent test coverage.
findings.rootCause: prisma-field-encryption library incompatible with Prisma 7's DMMF shape; replaced by a bespoke extension (client.ts:154-162) but the old test file was left half-disabled instead of being rewritten against the new implementation.
findings.policyViolation: ADR-054 flaky-test policy (linked-issue requirement — no issue ID present, only a code comment).
findings.remediation: Delete the describe.skip block testing the removed library (dead reference), and add direct unit coverage for the bespoke fieldEncryptionExtension() in client.ts instead of leaving 3 stale skipped cases.

findings.impact: This is a security-relevant sanitizer (command-injection pattern detection + PII redaction for AI prompts/outputs, per packages/adapters/src/shared/prompt-sanitizer.ts and its apps/api counterpart, both carrying eslint-disable annotations). Because it is duplicated rather than shared from one package, the two copies have already drifted on the exact regex used to detect shell-injection attempts, and apps/api has an entire PII-redaction function (sanitizeOutput) that packages/adapters lacks. A fix applied to one copy (e.g. patching a bypass) will silently NOT apply to the other, and different call sites in the monorepo get inconsistent protection.
findings.rootCause: No shared `@intelliflow/*` package owns prompt sanitization; the logic was copied into apps/api/src/shared and packages/adapters/src/shared independently and each has since evolved on its own.
findings.policyViolation: CLAUDE.md DRY / Shared Component Reuse Enforcement.
findings.remediation: Consolidate into a single source of truth (e.g. packages/validators or a new packages/security shared module), re-export from both current call sites, and add an architecture test (tests/architecture/) asserting no second copy of the sanitizer regex exists.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `wc -l apps/web/src/app/leads/[id]/page.tsx` = 2908 lines (largest non-generated, non-test TypeScript file in the repo). Line 1: `'use client';`. `grep -c "function \|const use\|useState\|useEffect" apps/web/src/app/leads/[id]/page.tsx` = 60 occurrences. Imports at lines 3-47 include useState/useMemo/useEffect/useRef, tRPC (`api`), auth (`useRequireAuth`), timezone context, server actions (`revalidateLeadCaches`), and 6+ shared UI components in one file.
findings.impact: A single client component combines data-fetching orchestration (tRPC hooks), business logic (avatar normalization, timezone conversion, cache revalidation), navigation, and rendering for the lead detail page in one 2908-line file. High cognitive load for changes, high risk of unrelated regressions when touching any one concern, and difficult to unit-test business logic in isolation from React rendering.
findings.rootCause: Feature grew incrementally in-place without extracting hooks (e.g. useLeadDetail) or splitting into container/presentational components.
findings.policyViolation: CLAUDE.md DRY / Shared Component Reuse Enforcement (implicit — no componentization discipline applied to this page).
findings.remediation: Extract data-fetching/business logic into custom hooks (e.g. useLeadDetailData, useLeadActions) and split rendering into smaller presentational components under apps/web/src/components/leads/detail/, following the pattern already used for tickets (apps/web/src/components/tickets/TicketDetail.tsx being closer to a reasonable ~1500 LOC ceiling).

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `wc -l apps/web/src/app/contacts/[id]/page.tsx` = 2575 lines. `grep -c "function \|const use\|useState\|useEffect" apps/web/src/app/contacts/[id]/page.tsx` = 48 occurrences.
findings.impact: Same god-component pattern as QUAL-016 for the contact detail page — data-fetching, business logic, and rendering combined in one 2575-line client component.
findings.rootCause: Same as QUAL-016; likely copy-pasted from the leads detail page given near-identical structure and import patterns.
findings.policyViolation: CLAUDE.md DRY / Shared Component Reuse Enforcement.
findings.remediation: Same remediation approach as QUAL-016; given the structural similarity, consider extracting a shared generic EntityDetailPage scaffold used by both leads and contacts detail pages to avoid maintaining two near-identical 2500+-line files.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: verbatim import lines 38-47: `import { api } from '@/lib/api';`, `import { useRequireAuth } from '@/lib/auth/AuthContext';`, `import { useTimezoneContext } from '@/providers/TimezoneProvider';`, `import { revalidateLeadCaches, revalidateLeadConversionCaches } from '../actions';`, `import { normalizeAvatarSource } from '@/lib/shared/avatar-utils';` — all co-located in the same 'use client' file as the JSX render tree, with 60 hook/function declarations (see QUAL-016).
findings.impact: Server-action cache revalidation, auth-gating, timezone conversion, and avatar-URL normalization (business/orchestration concerns) are interleaved with presentational JSX in one component, rather than being isolated behind hooks or a view-model layer. Makes it hard to unit-test the orchestration logic without mounting the full component tree, and increases blast radius when any single concern changes.
findings.rootCause: No container/presentation or hook-extraction convention enforced for Next.js detail pages in apps/web.
findings.policyViolation: CLAUDE.md apps/web/CLAUDE.md build validation / coverage scoping intent (implicit — untestable-without-full-render logic).
findings.remediation: Extract orchestration concerns (cache revalidation, auth gate, timezone formatting) into dedicated hooks that can be unit-tested independently of rendering; see QUAL-016 remediation.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `wc -l apps/api/src/modules/home/home.router.ts` = 2588 lines. `grep -c "publicProcedure\|protectedProcedure\|\.query(\|\.mutation(" apps/api/src/modules/home/home.router.ts` = 15.
findings.impact: A single tRPC router file at 2588 lines for only 15 procedures (~172 lines/procedure average) suggests substantial inline business logic (dashboard aggregation, widget config, etc.) that should live in an application-layer use case per the hexagonal architecture, not the router. Makes the router hard to navigate and couples HTTP/tRPC concerns to business logic.
findings.rootCause: Home-page aggregation logic (widgets, KPIs, activity feeds) implemented directly in router procedure bodies instead of delegated to packages/application use cases.
findings.policyViolation: CLAUDE.md architecture.md DDD/hexagonal boundary guidance (routers should be thin adapters over application use cases).
findings.remediation: Extract per-widget/per-section aggregation logic into packages/application use cases, leaving home.router.ts as thin input-validation + use-case-invocation wrappers.
findings.falsePositiveJustification: LOC-per-procedure average alone doesn't confirm business logic is inline vs. verbose Zod schemas/comments; a direct read of individual procedure bodies would be needed to confirm the split is warranted rather than just being a large but legitimately router-shaped file.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `wc -l apps/api/src/modules/contact/contact.router.ts` = 2555 lines. `grep -c "publicProcedure\|protectedProcedure\|\.query(\|\.mutation(" apps/api/src/modules/contact/contact.router.ts` = 28.
findings.impact: 2555-line router with 28 procedures (~91 lines/procedure). Lower LOC/procedure ratio than home.router.ts (QUAL-019) suggests this file is large primarily due to procedure count/breadth rather than inline logic concentration, but is still a maintainability outlier relative to the rest of the modules directory.
findings.rootCause: contact module has accumulated many fine-grained procedures over multiple sprints (IFC-255, IFC-265, IFC-266, IFC-270 per prior session memory) without periodic file-splitting.
findings.policyViolation: CLAUDE.md architecture.md DDD/hexagonal boundary guidance.
findings.remediation: Consider splitting into contact.router.ts (CRUD) + contact-audit.router.ts / contact-merge.router.ts sub-routers merged via mergeRouters, if procedure count keeps growing.
findings.falsePositiveJustification: 28 legitimate procedures in one bounded-context router may simply reflect a wide but cohesive API surface rather than a design smell; splitting could equally be seen as unnecessary fragmentation.

findings.impact: Test name claims to verify 'does NOT call adapter.logSecurityEvent' but the body never asserts on a mock/spy for logSecurityEvent — it only checks that the call didn't throw, then asserts a tautology. A regression where the no-adapter path silently DOES call a (now-defined) adapter's logSecurityEvent would not be caught by this test despite its name.
findings.rootCause: Test author conflated 'doesn't throw' with 'doesn't call the adapter'; no spy/mock was set up to actually verify the negative call assertion.
findings.remediation: Replace expect(true).toBe(true) with an actual spy assertion, e.g. a mock adapter with a logSecurityEvent vi.fn() asserted via expect(mockAdapter.logSecurityEvent).not.toHaveBeenCalled().

findings.impact: The test's real assertions (onChange called with correct isActive toggle) only run inside an `if (typeof onCheckedChange === 'function')` branch; the `else` branch silently passes via expect(true).toBe(true) instead of failing or being skipped explicitly. If the happy-dom environment stops propagating onCheckedChange (the exact scenario the comment anticipates), this test will report green while verifying nothing.
findings.rootCause: Conditional test logic based on a DOM-environment quirk (happy-dom Radix Switch prop propagation) instead of a deterministic test setup.
findings.remediation: Replace the runtime `if/else` branching with a deterministic Radix Switch test double that always exposes onCheckedChange, removing the fallback branch entirely; or use `it.skipIf(!supportsPropPropagation)` to make the environment dependency explicit instead of a tautological pass.

findings.impact: This test (and the four adjacent 'should apply X filter' tests at lines 277-333+) verifies TicketService.findMany builds the expected Prisma `where` clause, but never verifies the service actually returns filtered results — because the mock always resolves `[]` irrespective of input. A refactor that silently drops the status filter from the returned data (while still passing it to Prisma, e.g. a post-processing bug) would not be caught. This couples the test suite tightly to Prisma query-shape implementation details rather than TicketService's observable behavior.
findings.rootCause: Test-writing convention in this file favors asserting on the Prisma mock's call arguments over configuring mockResolvedValue to reflect the filter and asserting on the returned/filtered data.
findings.remediation: For each 'should apply X filter' test, configure mockPrisma.ticket.findMany.mockResolvedValue to return a mix of matching/non-matching fixture tickets and assert result.tickets reflects only the matching ones, in addition to (or instead of) the toHaveBeenCalledWith query-shape assertion.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `wc -l packages/db/prisma/cleanup-legacy-seed-ids.ts packages/db/prisma/__tests__/cleanup-legacy-seed-ids.test.ts packages/db/prisma/__tests__/cleanup-legacy-seed-ids.supplementary.test.ts packages/db/prisma/__tests__/cleanup-legacy-seed-ids.supplementary2.test.ts` = 305 (source) + 140 + 281 + 516 = 937 test lines across 3 files for a single 305-line script. `grep -n "^describe(" cleanup-legacy-seed-ids.supplementary.test.ts` shows 'cleanupLegacySeedData - comprehensive' (line 151) and 'showLegacyCounts - comprehensive' (line 99); `grep -n "^  describe(" cleanup-legacy-seed-ids.supplementary2.test.ts` shows 'cleanupLegacySeedData - all entity branches' (line 192) and 'showLegacyCounts - all 6 entity types' (line 298) — near-duplicate describe blocks re-testing the same two functions across two separate files.
findings.impact: A 305-line seed-maintenance script has 3.1x its own line count in test files, split across base/supplementary/supplementary2 suffixes with overlapping 'comprehensive' vs 'all branches' describe blocks for the same functions (cleanupLegacySeedData, showLegacyCounts, main()). This is coverage-chasing via file proliferation rather than one well-organized suite, making it hard to know which file to add a new case to and increasing the chance of the same scenario being asserted twice.
findings.rootCause: Each file was likely added in a separate work session to push coverage percentage up rather than consolidating into the existing cleanup-legacy-seed-ids.test.ts.
findings.policyViolation: CLAUDE.md DRY / Shared Component Reuse Enforcement (test-file variant).
findings.remediation: Merge cleanup-legacy-seed-ids.supplementary.test.ts and .supplementary2.test.ts into cleanup-legacy-seed-ids.test.ts, removing duplicate describe blocks and keeping the union of unique scenarios.

findings.evidence: REPRODUCIBLE: `wc -l packages/db/prisma/__tests__/seed-home-page.supplementary.test.ts packages/db/prisma/__tests__/seed-home-page.supplementary2.test.ts` = 304 + 540 = 844 lines across two 'supplementary'-suffixed files for one seed-home-page.ts script, following the identical naming/splitting pattern documented in QUAL-024.
findings.impact: Same test-suite-proliferation pattern as QUAL-024, applied to the home-page seed script.
findings.rootCause: Same as QUAL-024 — incremental coverage-chasing additions instead of consolidation.
findings.policyViolation: CLAUDE.md DRY / Shared Component Reuse Enforcement (test-file variant).
findings.remediation: Consolidate seed-home-page.supplementary.test.ts and .supplementary2.test.ts, following the same approach as QUAL-024.

findings.evidence:
REPRODUCIBLE: verbatim lines 219-223: "* Kept for backward compatibility.
 *
 * @deprecated Use publicProcedure instead — tracing is now applied globally.
 */
export const loggedProcedure = publicProcedure;". `grep -rl "loggedProcedure" apps/api/src/modules` → no files found (zero real router consumers). A second, differently-defined `loggedProcedure` also exists at apps/api/src/server.ts:77 (`export const loggedProcedure = t.procedure.use(loggingMiddleware);`), referenced only by trpc.ts, server.ts itself, and 3 test files (server.test.ts, trpc.test.ts, entry-points.test.ts) — never by any actual module router.
findings.impact: Two same-named but differently-implemented `loggedProcedure` exports exist (one a deprecated alias for publicProcedure, one an active logging-middleware wrapper), and neither is consumed by any of the 20+ router modules under apps/api/src/modules. This is dead/orphaned exported code that also creates a naming collision risk if a future router imports the wrong `loggedProcedure`.
findings.rootCause: Backward-compatibility export retained past the point where the last consumer was migrated to publicProcedure; the server.ts variant appears to be leftover from an earlier tracing/logging design.
findings.remediation: Remove the deprecated `loggedProcedure` export from apps/api/src/trpc.ts:223 (grep confirms zero consumers in apps/api/src/modules); confirm server.ts:77's version is also genuinely unused before removing it, updating or removing its 3 associated test files accordingly.
findings.falsePositiveJustification: loggedProcedure could be an intentionally-public API surface for future/external router authors even without current internal consumers; confirm via git blame / PR history before deleting.
